content.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an architectural pattern that enhances an LLM's responses by retrieving semantically relevant documents from an external knowledge corpus at query time, then presenting those documents alongside the user's question inside the prompt. Rather than relying entirely on knowledge baked into model weights during training, a RAG system maintains a living document index that can be updated without retraining the model. The enterprise RAG reference architecture specifies the complete set of components, integration contracts, and operational concerns required to deploy RAG reliably at organizational scale.
At the highest level the architecture splits into two pipelines. The
is an offline or near-real-time process that converts raw documents into searchable vector embeddings and stores them with associated metadata. The

is the real-time path that takes a user question, retrieves the most relevant chunks, optionally reranks them, assembles a prompt, and passes it to the LLM. These two pipelines share infrastructure — the vector store and the metadata database — but operate asynchronously, which is why document freshness and lifecycle management are first-class concerns in any enterprise deployment.

Enterprise RAG differs from prototype RAG in several important ways: it must support multiple business units (multi-tenancy), enforce access controls so users see only documents they are authorized to read, provide observability metrics that prove retrieval quality is high, and manage the lifecycle of evolving documents without requiring a full re-index on every change.

LLMs trained on general data cannot know about a company's internal knowledge base, recent policy documents, proprietary research, or real-time operational data. Without RAG, teams either fine-tune a model (expensive, slow, hard to keep current) or accept that the model will hallucinate answers drawn from stale or incorrect training data. RAG threads this needle: knowledge updates require only a re-ingest of the changed documents, not a new model training run.

For an enterprise legal team, RAG means contract analysis can reference the actual current clause library rather than a model's approximate memory of legal phrasing. For an IT help desk agent, RAG means the assistant can retrieve the exact configuration steps from the current runbook rather than reconstructing them from training. In both cases, the measurable impact is reduction in hallucination rate, which in enterprise settings translates directly to reduction in human escalation cost and risk exposure.

Hybrid search — combining dense vector similarity with sparse keyword matching (BM25) — addresses a real failure mode in dense-only retrieval: queries with precise proper nouns, product codes, or error message strings that do not have distinctive semantic signatures retrieve poorly with cosine similarity but perfectly with BM25. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) merges the two result lists without requiring score calibration, making it the practical default for enterprise corpora.

The model's effective knowledge updates on each document ingest rather than at retraining time. A policy document changed today is reflected in responses tonight after the incremental ingest job runs.
Grounding answers in retrieved text gives the model concrete content to paraphrase and cite, reducing the model's tendency to confabulate facts not in its training data.
Metadata filters applied at query time restrict retrieved chunks to documents the requesting user is authorized to read, without requiring separate model deployments per permission group.
Citations map every claim in the generated answer to a specific source document and page, which is required for regulated industries where auditability of AI outputs is mandatory.
Namespace isolation in the vector store and row-level access control in the metadata database prevent tenant A from retrieving tenant B's documents.
Only the top-k retrieved chunks (typically 1,500–3,000 tokens) are included in the prompt, keeping token costs predictable versus stuffing entire document corpora into the context window.
The document lifecycle manager handles content-hash deduplication so unchanged documents are never re-embedded, reducing embedding API costs for large corpora.

The LLM cannot answer better than the quality of what it retrieves. Poor chunking, mismatched embeddings, or a corpus that lacks the answer all produce incorrect or unhelpful responses regardless of model capability.

The full pipeline adds 200–800 ms over a direct LLM call: embedding the query, running hybrid retrieval, reranking, and assembling the prompt each contribute. Reranker inference is the most expensive stage.
Retrieving many chunks to improve recall eventually collides with the model's context window. Retrieving 20 chunks at 512 tokens each occupies 10K tokens before the question is included.
A document changed between ingestion runs has stale embeddings in the vector store. The ingestion job frequency must match the acceptable staleness SLA for each document category.

Chunk size and overlap are hyperparameters that interact with embedding model token limits and the nature of the corpus. Values that work for wiki articles may perform poorly for dense legal prose or code files.

Enterprise RAG requires operating a vector store, a metadata database, an embedding service, an ingestion pipeline, a reranker, and an observability stack — each with its own availability, scaling, and cost profile.

The ingestion pipeline begins when a document event (new upload, content edit, deletion) triggers the pipeline. The document is loaded and pre-processed: HTML stripped, headers normalized, tables extracted. The text is split into overlapping chunks using a recursive character splitter; each chunk inherits the document's metadata (source URL, author, last-modified timestamp, tenant ID, ACL group list). Each chunk is sent to the embedding model to produce a dense vector. The chunk text and its metadata are written to the metadata database; the dense vector is written to the vector store, keyed by a content-hash-derived ID. If a previous version of the document exists, its chunk IDs are soft-deleted from the vector store.

The query pipeline begins at the API gateway where the request is authenticated and the caller's tenant context and permission groups are extracted. The user's natural-language question optionally passes through a query rewriting step — HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) generates a hypothetical answer whose embedding is used instead of the question's embedding, improving recall for open-ended questions; multi-query generates several rephrasings and merges their result lists. The rewritten query is embedded with the same model used during ingestion, then sent to both the vector store (dense ANN search) and the BM25 index (sparse keyword search). Reciprocal Rank Fusion combines the two ranked lists: each document's score is

Multi-tenancy is enforced at two points. In the vector store, each tenant's documents are stored in a dedicated namespace (Pinecone) or under a filtered collection (pgvector) so ANN search cannot return cross-tenant results even if the namespace filter were accidentally omitted. In the metadata database, row-level access control ensures that BM25 search and the document lifecycle manager only operate on documents the requesting tenant owns.

attrs.alt: Enterprise RAG reference architecture showing ingestion pipeline (Document Sources → Chunking → Embedding → Vector Store → Metadata DB → Doc Lifecycle), query pipeline (User Query → Query Rewriting → Hybrid Retrieval → Reranker → Context Assembly → LLM Generation), and cross-cutting concerns (Retrieval Observability, Latency Tracing, Multi-Tenancy Controls, Security Layer)
attrs.title: Enterprise RAG reference architecture showing ingestion pipeline (Document Sources → Chunking → Embedding → Vector Store → Metadata DB → Doc Lifecycle), query pipeline (User Query → Query Rewriting → Hybrid Retrieval → Reranker → Context Assembly → LLM Generation), and cross-cutting concerns (Retrieval Observability, Latency Tracing, Multi-Tenancy Controls, Security Layer)

content.text: The ingestion pipeline (top row, blue) flows left to right from document sources through chunking, embedding, vector store, metadata database, and document lifecycle manager. The query pipeline (second row, green/amber) similarly flows left to right, drawing from both the vector store and metadata database during hybrid retrieval. The LLM generation stage sits at the right end of the query pipeline. The bottom band shows cross-cutting concerns — observability, multi-tenancy, and security — that span both pipelines. The component decision matrix (bottom panel) lists recommended tooling choices at each stage.

Use enterprise RAG whenever an agent must answer questions about a corpus that changes more frequently than the model retraining cadence, or that contains proprietary information not present in the model's training data. Canonical use cases: internal knowledge bases, technical documentation Q&A, contract review, regulatory compliance assistance, customer support powered by product documentation.
the answer must be traceable to a specific source document for audit purposes.
the corpus changes weekly or more frequently — fine-tuning cannot keep pace.
different user groups must see different subsets of the knowledge base.

the task requires multi-hop reasoning across dozens of documents simultaneously — the context window will be overwhelmed before retrieval covers the needed breadth. Consider a graph RAG or agent-based approach.
the question is fully answerable from the model's training data and low latency is critical — the additional pipeline latency is unnecessary overhead.
Define your document taxonomy and metadata schema (tenant ID, source system, ACL groups, content type) before writing any ingestion code.

Choose your chunking strategy. Start with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 512 tokens / 64 overlap. After baseline evaluation, experiment with semantic chunking for narrative prose or sentence-window retrieval for dense technical documents.
Select your embedding model. text-embedding-3-large is a strong default for English corpora; BGE-M3 is the best open-source option for multilingual content.
Set up the vector store with namespace partitioning per tenant. For SQL-native shops, pgvector co-located with your existing PostgreSQL is the lowest-operational-overhead choice.
Add BM25 alongside the vector store from day one — do not plan to add sparse search later; retrofitting it to an existing pipeline requires reprocessing the entire corpus.
Integrate the reranker at the end of retrieval. Set top_k=20 for retrieval and top_n=5 for the reranker as starting values.
Build the document lifecycle manager that tracks content hashes and propagates deletions and updates to the vector store.
Add observability from the first week: instrument retrieval latency as an OpenTelemetry span and track context recall and faithfulness using RAGAS on a golden eval set.

# Enterprise RAG pipeline with hybrid search (LangChain + pgvector)
# pip install langchain langchain-openai langchain-community psycopg2-binary cohere
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores.pgvector import PGVector
from langchain.retrievers import EnsembleRetriever, ContextualCompressionRetriever
from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain.retrievers.document_compressors import CohereRerank
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain.schema import Document
import os

CONN = os.environ["PG_CONN"]  # postgresql://user:pass@host/db
COLLECTION = "enterprise_docs"

def build_vector_store(docs: list[Document], tenant_id: str) -> PGVector:
    """Ingest documents for a specific tenant using namespace isolation."""
    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64,
        separators=["\n\n", "\n", ".", " ", ""],
    )
    chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)
    # Inject tenant metadata for row-level filtering
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunk.metadata["tenant_id"] = tenant_id
    embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-large")
    return PGVector.from_documents(
        documents=chunks,
        embedding=embeddings,
        collection_name=COLLECTION,
        connection_string=CONN,
        pre_delete_collection=False,
    )

def build_rag_chain(tenant_id: str):
    """Build a hybrid RAG chain with reranking and tenant isolation."""
    embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-large")
    vector_store = PGVector(
        collection_name=COLLECTION,
        connection_string=CONN,
        embedding_function=embeddings,
    )
    # Dense retriever with metadata filter
    dense_retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(
        search_kwargs={"k": 20, "filter": {"tenant_id": tenant_id}},
    )
    # Sparse retriever built from corpus loaded per-tenant
    corpus_docs = load_tenant_corpus(tenant_id)  # returns list[Document]
    bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_documents(corpus_docs, k=20)

    # Hybrid: RRF fusion of dense + sparse
    ensemble = EnsembleRetriever(
        retrievers=[dense_retriever, bm25_retriever],
        weights=[0.6, 0.4],  # tune for your domain
    )
    # Reranker compresses to top-5
    compressor = CohereRerank(model="rerank-english-v3.0", top_n=5)
    retriever = ContextualCompressionRetriever(
        base_compressor=compressor, base_retriever=ensemble
    )
    prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
        "Answer using ONLY the provided context. Cite source URLs.\n\n"
        "Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"
    )
    llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
    return (
        {"context": retriever | format_docs, "question": RunnablePassthrough()}
        | prompt | llm | StrOutputParser()
    )

def format_docs(docs: list[Document]) -> str:
    return "\n\n".join(
        f"[{d.metadata.get('source','?')}]\n{d.page_content}" for d in docs
    )

def load_tenant_corpus(tenant_id: str) -> list[Document]:
    # Stub: load from DB; replace with real query
    return []
Full RAG Pipeline with Hybrid Search
The code above shows a production LangChain hybrid RAG chain. Below is the document lifecycle manager:

# Document lifecycle management: versioning, deletion, re-indexing
import hashlib, time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class DocVersion:
    doc_id: str
    version: int
    content_hash: str
    chunk_ids: list[str]          # vector store IDs to delete on supersede
    created_at: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    deleted_at: Optional[float] = None

class DocumentLifecycleManager:
    """Track document versions and keep vector store in sync."""

    def __init__(self, vector_store, metadata_db):
        self.vs = vector_store
        self.db = metadata_db  # e.g., SQLAlchemy session

    def _hash(self, content: str) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    def upsert(self, doc_id: str, content: str, metadata: dict) -> DocVersion:
        new_hash = self._hash(content)
        existing = self.db.get_latest_version(doc_id)
        if existing and existing.content_hash == new_hash:
            return existing  # content unchanged, skip re-index
        new_chunks = self._ingest(doc_id, content, metadata)
        version = (existing.version + 1) if existing else 1
        if existing:
            # Soft-delete old chunk embeddings
            self.vs.delete(ids=existing.chunk_ids)
            existing.deleted_at = time.time()
            self.db.save(existing)
        doc_ver = DocVersion(
            doc_id=doc_id, version=version,
            content_hash=new_hash, chunk_ids=new_chunks,
        )
        self.db.save(doc_ver)
        return doc_ver

    def delete(self, doc_id: str) -> None:
        ver = self.db.get_latest_version(doc_id)
        if ver:
            self.vs.delete(ids=ver.chunk_ids)
            ver.deleted_at = time.time()
            self.db.save(ver)

    def _ingest(self, doc_id, content, metadata) -> list[str]:
        # Chunk, embed, index — returns list of new vector IDs
        raise NotImplementedError  # wire to build_vector_store helper

A global law firm deployed enterprise RAG to handle contract review queries across a corpus of 4 million clause library entries owned by 12 business units. Each business unit is a tenant; paralegals from one unit must not retrieve another unit's proprietary clause language. The ingestion pipeline runs every 15 minutes using an event-driven trigger from SharePoint webhooks. The p99 retrieval latency including reranking is 420 ms; overall query-to-response latency is 1.1 s. Context recall (measured nightly by RAGAS against 400 golden QA pairs) is 0.87, and faithfulness is 0.93.
The RAGAS evaluation framework measures four key metrics:
(do the retrieved chunks cover the answer?),
(is the generated answer grounded in the retrieved context?), and
(does the answer address the question?). Running this suite nightly against a golden evaluation set provides an early warning when a corpus change or model update degrades retrieval quality.
Chunking Too Large or Too Small

Chunks of 2,000+ tokens frequently contain multiple distinct topics, degrading embedding quality because a single vector must represent heterogeneous content. Chunks under 128 tokens often lack enough context for the reranker to judge relevance. Start at 400–512 tokens and validate with your retrieval metrics.

Hybrid retrieval without reranking returns top-20 chunks to the prompt, flooding the context window with marginally relevant content. The reranker's cross-encoder scores (query, chunk) pairs jointly, producing a significantly higher-quality top-5 than any bi-encoder ANN search can achieve alone. The reranker adds 50–150 ms but consistently improves faithfulness scores by 0.05–0.15.

Deleting a document from SharePoint or S3 does not automatically remove its embeddings from the vector store. Without a lifecycle manager tracking chunk IDs, deleted documents continue to be retrieved indefinitely. Always propagate deletion events from the source system to the vector store.

Pure vector search performs poorly on queries with specific product codes, error strings, or proper nouns that lack semantic neighbors in the embedding space. Adding BM25 as the sparse retrieval leg and fusing with RRF costs little extra operational overhead and meaningfully improves recall for keyword-heavy queries.
Measure context recall and context precision on a golden eval set before tuning the generation prompt. If retrieval is poor, prompt tuning cannot compensate.
Switching embedding models requires re-embedding the entire corpus. Treat the embedding model as a versioned artifact; bump the version only with a full re-ingest.
Add OpenTelemetry spans to chunking, embedding, ANN search, BM25 search, reranking, and prompt assembly. Stage-level p99 latency reveals where optimization effort pays off.
Use content hashes for idempotent ingestion.
Hash the document content before ingesting; skip re-embedding if the hash has not changed. This makes the ingestion pipeline safe to re-run on the full corpus without unnecessary embedding API calls.
Enforce ACL at the retrieval layer, not the generation layer.
The LLM cannot reliably suppress information present in its context window. Filter unauthorized documents before they enter the prompt, not after.
Every vector chunk ID must map back to a document ID, version, page number, and source URL. Without this mapping, citations in generated answers cannot be verified.

At 1,000 queries per minute, the bottleneck is typically the reranker inference: cross-encoder models run at 20–100 ms per (query, chunk) pair, and scoring 20 candidates requires parallel batching or a faster model. Hosting a BGE-reranker-v2-m3 on a single A10G GPU reduces reranker latency to under 30 ms for a batch of 20 candidates.

Embedding the query is the second bottleneck. The OpenAI text-embedding-3-large endpoint has a median latency of 60–100 ms. For low-latency requirements, deploy a self-hosted BGE-M3 or a distilled embedding model on a CPU instance adjacent to the application server.

Pinecone and Weaviate scale query throughput horizontally by adding replica pods. pgvector performance degrades above ~5 million vectors on a single node; partition by tenant or use the pgvectorscale extension for high-cardinality deployments.

Process ingestion events in parallel using a worker queue (Celery, Kafka consumer group). Each worker handles one document end-to-end. Aim for ingestion throughput 10× higher than the peak document change rate to maintain a small freshness lag.
Cache the query embedding for repeated identical queries using a Redis LRU cache keyed on the query string. Cache the reranked top-k results for a short TTL (30–60 s) for high-traffic Q&A scenarios.
Mature orchestration framework with built-in retrievers, document loaders for 50+ sources, and EnsembleRetriever for hybrid search. The

Retrieval-first framework with richer built-in chunking strategies (sentence-window, hierarchical), a query transformation library, and tighter integration with llamaparse for complex PDF/table extraction.
Pipeline-oriented framework with composable components suitable for production RAG systems that need explainable pipeline graphs and unit-testable components.
Offline evaluation framework for RAG. Implements reference-free metrics (faithfulness, answer relevancy) and reference-based metrics (context precision, context recall) using an LLM judge.
Open-source LLM observability platform with native RAG tracing support. Captures retrieval spans, reranker scores, and prompt templates alongside model generation traces.
PostgreSQL extension for vector similarity search. pgvectorscale adds the DiskANN index for sub-linear ANN search at tens of millions of vectors on commodity hardware.

# Quick start: pgvector hybrid retrieval
# CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;
# CREATE TABLE chunks (
#   id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
#   tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL,
#   doc_id TEXT NOT NULL,
#   content TEXT,
#   embedding vector(3072),  -- text-embedding-3-large dim
#   metadata JSONB,
#   ts_content tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', content)) STORED
# );
# CREATE INDEX ON chunks USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists = 100);
# CREATE INDEX ON chunks USING GIN (ts_content);

# Dense query (per-tenant):
# SELECT id, content, 1 - (embedding <=> $1::vector) AS score
# FROM chunks WHERE tenant_id = $2
# ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT 20;

# Sparse BM25 query:
# SELECT id, content, ts_rank(ts_content, plainto_tsquery($1)) AS score
# FROM chunks WHERE tenant_id = $2
# ORDER BY ts_rank DESC LIMIT 20;

Access control enforcement must happen at the retrieval layer. If unauthorized documents appear in the retrieved context, the LLM will incorporate their content into the response regardless of any instruction to ignore them. Metadata filters in the vector store query and row-level ACL in the metadata database are the correct controls; prompt-level instructions like "do not reveal confidential documents" are not a reliable security boundary.
Run a PII detection pass on document chunks before embedding. Redact or tokenize PII before storing in the vector store so retrieval cannot surface raw personal data.

Malicious documents can contain instructions designed to hijack the agent ("ignore previous instructions and..."). Sanitize retrieved chunks by stripping known injection patterns before inserting them into the prompt.
Log every retrieval event — query, tenant ID, retrieved chunk IDs, timestamp — to an append-only audit trail. This is required for SOC 2 and GDPR compliance.
Encrypt vectors at rest and in transit. Vectors themselves can leak information about document content through embedding inversion attacks.

An attacker with write access to the ingestion pipeline can inject crafted documents that manipulate retrieval for target queries. Validate and authenticate document sources; restrict write access to the ingestion pipeline with tight IAM controls.

Think of enterprise RAG as a very fast, very accurate research librarian embedded inside the LLM's prompt. When you ask a question, the librarian does not guess from memory — she runs to the stacks, pulls exactly the three most relevant pages, hands them to the LLM, and says "answer only from these." The LLM's job is synthesis and language, not memory. The librarian's job is retrieval precision.

The ingestion pipeline is the librarian cataloguing new books as they arrive — assigning call numbers (chunk IDs), writing index cards (embeddings), and noting which reading rooms have access to which sections (ACL metadata). The hybrid search is the librarian consulting both the card catalogue (BM25) and her personal mental map of conceptually related books (vector search) before selecting candidates. The reranker is the librarian re-reading the top candidates to decide which three are truly most useful for this specific question.

Enterprise RAG has two distinct pipelines: an offline ingestion pipeline that converts documents into searchable embeddings, and a real-time query pipeline that retrieves and ranks those embeddings before passing context to the LLM.
Hybrid search (dense ANN + BM25 with Reciprocal Rank Fusion) consistently outperforms dense-only retrieval on enterprise corpora that contain proper nouns, product codes, and error strings.

The reranker is not optional for production quality: cross-encoder reranking of the top-20 retrieved chunks to a top-5 compressed set is the single highest-leverage improvement over a baseline RAG implementation.

Multi-tenancy requires enforcement at two layers: namespace isolation in the vector store prevents cross-tenant ANN search contamination; row-level ACL in the metadata database prevents cross-tenant BM25 and lifecycle operations.

Document lifecycle management must track content hashes, chunk IDs, and version history so that document updates and deletions propagate cleanly to the vector store without stale embeddings accumulating.

Observability is not optional: context recall, context precision, faithfulness, and answer relevancy measured nightly against a golden eval set are the leading indicators of RAG system health before user complaints surface.
Embedding model version must be treated as a versioned artifact: switching models requires re-embedding the entire corpus, so pin the model version and plan model upgrades as migration projects.
